# Exodus Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~41r–42r (PDF pp. 77–79); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 55–56 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_015_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — SPECIAL SHIRAH (SONG) LAYOUT:** The Leningrad Codex writes the Song of the Sea (vv. 1b–19) in the distinctive "brick-over-half-brick" (ariakh al gabei ariakh) layout prescribed by the Talmud (Megillah 16b) for this passage. Each line of the Song is arranged in three columns — text, space, text — with the text blocks of successive lines offset like bricks in a wall. This special scribal layout (shirat ha-yam) is one of only two passages in the Torah given this treatment (the other being the Song of Deborah, Judges 5). The layout visually distinguishes the poetic text from the surrounding prose narrative and signals its liturgical importance. The Song is preceded by a brief prose introduction (v. 1a) in standard column format, and the prose resumes at v. 19. The Masoretic paragraph markers (setumah/petuchah) frame the Song as a self-contained literary unit. This is clearly visible in pages 001 and 002 of the LC images, where the distinctive three-column brick layout contrasts sharply with the standard prose columns that precede (end of ch. 14) and follow (vv. 19ff).

---

## The Song of Mosheh — Shirat ha-Yam (vv. 1–19)

**1** Then Mosheh sang [yashir], and the sons of Yisra'el, this shirah [song] to YHWH; and they said, saying:

"I will sing [ashirah] to YHWH, for he has risen in triumph [ga'oh ga'ah] — the horse [sus] and its rider [rokhevo] He has hurled [ramah] into the sea [ba-yam]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אָז יָשִׁיר־מֹשֶׁה וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַשִּׁירָה הַזֹּאת לַיהוָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ לֵאמֹר אָשִׁירָה לַיהוָה כִּי־גָאֹה גָּאָה סוּס וְרֹכְבוֹ רָמָה בַיָּם (az yashir-Mosheh u-venei Yisra'el et-ha-shirah ha-zot la-YHWH vayomeru lemor ashirah la-YHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus ve-rokhevo ramah va-yam, "then Mosheh sang, and the sons of Yisra'el, this song to YHWH; and they said, saying: 'I will sing to YHWH, for he has risen in triumph — the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the oldest extended poem in the Torah:** The verb יָשִׁיר (yashir) = yiqtol (imperfect) of שׁיר (shir, "to sing"), here functioning as a preterite/narrative past: "then he sang." The use of אָז (az, "then") + yiqtol is a distinctive archaic Hebrew construction for past narration, preserved in early poetry (cf. Num 21:17; Josh 10:12; 1 Kgs 8:12). The form אָשִׁירָה (ashirah) = cohortative of שׁיר — "I will sing! / Let me sing!" — expressing the singer's volition and determination. The phrase כִּי־גָאֹה גָּאָה (ki-ga'oh ga'ah) = cognate accusative (infinitive absolute + perfect) of גאה (ga'ah, "to rise up, be exalted, be majestic") — "for rising up He has risen up" = "He has triumphed gloriously / He has risen in triumph." The doubling intensifies: YHWH's victory is absolute. The verb רָמָה (ramah) = "He has hurled, He has cast" — from רמה (ramah, "to throw, hurl, cast"). Not merely "drowned" but violently thrown — the horse and rider were flung into the sea by divine force. LXX: τότε ᾖσεν Μωυσῆς καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τὴν ᾠδὴν ταύτην τῷ θεῷ καὶ εἶπαν λέγοντες· ᾄσωμεν τῷ κυρίῳ, ἐνδόξως γὰρ δεδόξασται· ἵππον καὶ ἀναβάτην ἔρριψεν εἰς θάλασσαν ("then Mosheh sang, and the sons of Israel, this song to God and said, saying: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for He has been gloriously glorified; horse and rider He has cast into the sea'") — ἐνδόξως δεδόξασται ("gloriously He has been glorified") for ga'oh ga'ah, a striking interpretive rendering that shifts from "risen in triumph" to "glorified"; ῥίπτω ("throw, cast") for ramah.

**2** My strength [ozi] and my song [ve-zimrat] — Yah! And He has become for me yeshu'ah [salvation]. This [is] my El [God], and I will make Him a habitation [ve-anvehu]; the Elohei [God of] my father, and I will exalt Him [va-aromemenhu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads עָזִּי וְזִמְרָת יָהּ וַיְהִי־לִי לִישׁוּעָה זֶה אֵלִי וְאַנְוֵהוּ אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי וַאֲרֹמְמֶנְהוּ (ozi ve-zimrat Yah vayehi-li li-yeshu'ah zeh Eli ve-anvehu Elohei avi va-aromemenhu, "my strength and my song — Yah! And He has become for me salvation. This [is] my God, and I will make Him a habitation; the God of my father, and I will exalt Him"). **TEXTUAL CRUX — zimrat or zimrati?** The word וְזִמְרָת (ve-zimrat) is a construct form: "and [the] song of" — reading "my strength and the song of Yah." Some scholars emend to וְזִמְרָתִי (ve-zimrati) = "and my song [is] Yah," reading a first-person suffix. The LC clearly reads zimrat (construct) without the yod suffix. The parallel in Isaiah 12:2 and Psalm 118:14 has the same form. The construct reading yields: "my strength and the fierce-song of Yah" — where zimrah connotes not merely song but powerful, triumphant music. **Key terms:** עָזִּי (ozi) = "my strength" — from עֹז (oz, "strength, might"). יָהּ (Yah) = the short form of the divine name YHWH, used primarily in poetry and in the exclamation הַלְלוּ־יָהּ (hallelu-Yah). זֶה אֵלִי (zeh Eli) = "this [is] my God" — the demonstrative pronoun points to the God whose act was just witnessed at the sea; a finger pointing at the divine warrior. The verb וְאַנְוֵהוּ (ve-anvehu) is debated: (a) from נוה (navah, "to dwell, make a habitation") = "I will make Him a dwelling / I will beautify His habitation"; (b) from נאה (na'ah, "to be beautiful, seemly") = "I will beautify/glorify Him." The Targumim and many rabbis take it as "I will build Him a sanctuary." וַאֲרֹמְמֶנְהוּ (va-aromemenhu) = polel of רום (rum, "to be high, exalted") + 3ms suffix — "and I will exalt Him." LXX: βοηθὸς καὶ σκεπαστὴς ἐγένετό μοι εἰς σωτηρίαν· οὗτος μου θεός, καὶ δοξάσω αὐτόν, θεὸς τοῦ πατρός μου, καὶ ὑψώσω αὐτόν ("helper and protector He became for me unto salvation; this [is] my God, and I will glorify Him, God of my father, and I will exalt Him") — βοηθός ("helper") and σκεπαστής ("protector") for ozi ve-zimrat Yah — an expansive, interpretive rendering that replaces the Hebrew poetic compression with explicit theological titles.

**3** YHWH [is] a man of war [ish milkhamah]; YHWH [is] His name [shemo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads יְהוָה אִישׁ מִלְחָמָה יְהוָה שְׁמוֹ (YHWH ish milkhamah YHWH shemo, "YHWH [is] a man of war; YHWH [is] His name"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the divine warrior:** The declaration אִישׁ מִלְחָמָה (ish milkhamah) = "man of war / warrior" — from אִישׁ (ish, "man") + מִלְחָמָה (milkhamah, "war, battle," from לחם, lakham, "to fight"). YHWH is identified not metaphorically but directly as a warrior. This is the foundational statement of the Divine Warrior motif that pervades the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ps 24:8; Isa 42:13; Zeph 3:17). The second clause יְהוָה שְׁמוֹ (YHWH shemo) = "YHWH [is] His name" — a self-identification formula. The warrior has a name: YHWH, the covenant God who revealed Himself to Mosheh at the bush (3:14–15). He is not an anonymous cosmic force but a named, personal deity who fights for His people. LXX: κύριος συντρίβων πολέμους, κύριος ὄνομα αὐτῷ ("the Lord shattering wars, the Lord [is] His name") — a striking divergence: the LXX renders "man of war" as "one who shatters/crushes wars," transforming YHWH from a warrior to a peace-bringer who destroys warfare itself. This may reflect Hellenistic Jewish theological sensibility.

**4** The chariots [markevot] of Par'oh and his army [kheilo] He has cast [yarah] into the sea; and the choice [mivkhar] of his shalishim [officers] are sunk [tubba'u] in the Yam Suf [Sea of Reeds].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מַרְכְּבֹת פַּרְעֹה וְחֵילוֹ יָרָה בַיָּם וּמִבְחַר שָׁלִשָׁיו טֻבְּעוּ בְיַם־סוּף (markevot Par'oh ve-kheilo yarah va-yam u-mivkhar shalishav tubba'u ve-Yam-Suf, "the chariots of Par'oh and his army He has cast into the sea; and the choice of his officers are sunk in the Sea of Reeds"). **Key terms:** מַרְכְּבֹת (markevot) = plural of מֶרְכָּבָה (merkavah, "chariot") — from רכב (rakhav, "to ride"). יָרָה (yarah) = "He has cast, He has hurled" — from ירה (yarah, "to throw, cast, shoot"). מִבְחַר (mivkhar) = "the choice, the choicest, the pick of" — from בחר (bakhar, "to choose"). שָׁלִשָׁיו (shalishav) = "his officers" — the שָׁלִישׁ (shalish, "third-man, officer") with 3ms suffix. טֻבְּעוּ (tubba'u) = pual of טבע (tava, "to sink") — "they were sunk, they were drowned." The passive emphasizes divine agency — they did not merely drown; they were sunk. יַם־סוּף (Yam-Suf) = "Sea of Reeds" — from סוּף (suf, "reed, rush"). LXX: ἅρματα Φαραω καὶ τὴν δύναμιν αὐτοῦ ἔρριψεν εἰς θάλασσαν, ἐπιλέκτους ἀναβάτας τριστάτας κατεπόντισεν ἐν ἐρυθρᾷ θαλάσσῃ ("chariots of Pharao and his force He has cast into the sea; chosen mounted officers He has sunk in the Red Sea") — ἐρυθρὰ θάλασσα ("Red Sea") for Yam Suf — the famous LXX rendering that became standard in Western tradition.

**5** The tehomot [deeps] cover them [yekhaseyumu]; they went down [yaredu] into the depths [metsolot] like a stone [ke-aven].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads תְּהֹמֹת יְכַסְיֻמוּ יָרְדוּ בִמְצוֹלֹת כְּמוֹ־אָבֶן (tehomot yekhaseyumu yaredu vi-metsolot kemo-aven, "the deeps cover them; they went down into the depths like a stone"). **Key terms:** תְּהֹמֹת (tehomot) = plural of תְּהוֹם (tehom, "deep, abyss") — the primordial deep, recalling Genesis 1:2 (tehom, "the deep"). The same cosmic waters that existed before creation now serve as YHWH's weapon. יְכַסְיֻמוּ (yekhaseyumu) = piel of כסה (kasah, "to cover") + 3mp suffix — "they cover them." מְצוֹלֹת (metsolot) = plural of מְצוֹלָה (metsolah, "depth, deep place") — from צול/צלל (tsul/tsalal, "to sink, be deep"). כְּמוֹ־אָבֶן (kemo-aven) = "like a stone" — the first of three similes for the sinking Egyptians (stone here, stubble in v. 7, lead in v. 10). The progression from stone to lead suggests increasing weight and speed of descent. LXX: πόντῳ ἐκάλυψεν αὐτούς, κατέδυσαν εἰς βυθὸν ὡσεὶ λίθος ("with the open sea He covered them; they sank into the deep like a stone") — πόντος ("open sea") for tehomot; βυθός ("depth of the sea") for metsolot.

**6** Your right hand [yeminekha], O YHWH, is glorious [ne'dari] in power [ba-koakh]; your right hand, O YHWH, shatters [tir'ats] the enemy [oyev].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads יְמִינְךָ יְהוָה נֶאְדָּרִי בַּכֹּחַ יְמִינְךָ יְהוָה תִּרְעַץ אוֹיֵב (yeminekha YHWH ne'dari ba-koakh yeminekha YHWH tir'ats oyev, "your right hand, O YHWH, is glorious in power; your right hand, O YHWH, shatters the enemy"). **Key terms:** יְמִינְךָ (yeminekha) = "your right hand" — from יָמִין (yamin, "right hand"), the hand of power, authority, and favor. The repetition of "your right hand, O YHWH" (anaphora) is a hallmark of archaic Hebrew poetry, creating rhythmic emphasis. נֶאְדָּרִי (ne'dari) = niphal participle of אדר (adar, "to be glorious, majestic") — "glorious, majestic." The form with final yod is archaic (cf. the archaic -i ending in early Hebrew poetry). תִּרְעַץ (tir'ats) = qal imperfect of רעץ (ra'ats, "to shatter, break, crush") — "shatters, crushes." The verb denotes violent destruction. אוֹיֵב (oyev) = "enemy" — the active participle of איב (ayav, "to be hostile"). LXX: ἡ δεξιά σου, κύριε, δεδόξασται ἐν ἰσχύι, ἡ δεξιά σου χείρ, κύριε, ἔθραυσεν ἐχθρούς ("your right hand, O Lord, has been glorified in strength; your right hand, O Lord, has shattered enemies") — δοξάζω ("glorify") for ne'dari; θραύω ("shatter, break") for tir'ats.

**7** And in the greatness [u-ve-rov] of your majesty [ge'onekha] you tear down [taharos] those rising against you [qamekha]; you send forth [teshalakh] your burning wrath [kharonekha] — it consumes them [yokhlemo] like stubble [ka-qash].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבְרֹב גְּאוֹנְךָ תַּהֲרֹס קָמֶיךָ תְּשַׁלַּח חֲרֹנְךָ יֹאכְלֵמוֹ כַּקַּשׁ (u-ve-rov ge'onekha taharos qamekha teshalakh kharonekha yokhlemo ka-qash, "and in the greatness of your majesty you tear down those rising against you; you send forth your burning wrath — it consumes them like stubble"). **Key terms:** גְּאוֹן (ge'on) = "majesty, exaltation, pride" — from גאה (ga'ah, "to rise up, be exalted"), the same root as ga'oh ga'ah in v. 1. YHWH's ga'on is His rising-up-ness, His towering majesty. תַּהֲרֹס (taharos) = qal imperfect of הרס (haras, "to tear down, demolish, overthrow") — the verb used for demolishing walls and buildings, here applied to enemies. קָמֶיךָ (qamekha) = "those rising against you" — from קום (qum, "to rise, stand up") + 2ms suffix; the participial form denoting adversaries. חָרוֹן (kharon) = "burning anger, fierce wrath" — from חרה (kharah, "to burn, be kindled"). יֹאכְלֵמוֹ (yokhlemo) = qal imperfect of אכל (akhal, "to eat, consume") + 3mp suffix — "it eats/consumes them." כַּקַּשׁ (ka-qash) = "like the stubble/chaff" — קַשׁ (qash, "stubble, chaff, dry straw"), the remnant of grain after threshing, easily consumed by fire (cf. Isa 5:24; Nah 1:10). LXX: καὶ τῷ πλήθει τῆς δόξης σου συνέτριψας τοὺς ὑπεναντίους· ἀπέστειλας τὸν θυμόν σου, κατέφαγεν αὐτοὺς ὡς καλάμην ("and in the multitude of your glory you shattered the adversaries; you sent forth your wrath, it consumed them like chaff") — καλάμη ("stubble, chaff") for qash.

**8** And by the ruakh [wind/breath/spirit] of your nostrils [appekha] the waters [mayim] were piled up [ne'ermu]; the flowing-waters [nozelim] stood firm [nitsavu] like a heap [kemo-ned]; the tehomot [deeps] congealed [qafe'u] in the lev [heart] of the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבְרוּחַ אַפֶּיךָ נֶעֶרְמוּ מַיִם נִצְּבוּ כְמוֹ־נֵד נֹזְלִים קָפְאוּ תְהֹמֹת בְּלֶב־יָם (u-ve-ruakh appekha ne'ermu mayim nitsavu kemo-ned nozelim qafe'u tehomot be-lev-yam, "and by the wind/breath of your nostrils the waters were piled up; the flowing-waters stood firm like a heap; the deeps congealed in the heart of the sea"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — ruakh appekha:** The phrase רוּחַ אַפֶּיךָ (ruakh appekha) = "the wind/breath/spirit of your nostrils" — from רוּחַ (ruakh, "wind, breath, spirit") + אַף (af, "nostril, nose, anger"; dual: אַפַּיִם, appayim). The "blast of God's nostrils" is the east wind of 14:21, now mythopoeticized — the natural phenomenon revealed as divine exhalation. The same ruakh that hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2 now piles them up. נֶעֶרְמוּ (ne'ermu) = niphal of ערם (aram, "to heap up, pile up") — "were heaped up." כְמוֹ־נֵד (kemo-ned) = "like a heap, like a wall" — from נד (ned, "heap, wall of water"). The image is of water standing vertically, defying nature. נֹזְלִים (nozelim) = "flowing waters, streams" — from נזל (nazal, "to flow, trickle, stream"); the participle indicates what should be flowing but has frozen in place. קָפְאוּ (qafe'u) = qal perfect of קפא (qafa, "to congeal, thicken, curdle, freeze") — the deep waters solidified like ice or curdled milk. בְּלֶב־יָם (be-lev-yam) = "in the heart of the sea" — לֵב (lev, "heart") used for the innermost, central part. LXX: καὶ διὰ πνεύματος τοῦ θυμοῦ σου διέστη τὸ ὕδωρ· ἐπάγη ὡσεὶ τεῖχος τὰ ὕδατα, ἐπάγη τὰ κύματα ἐν μέσῳ τῆς θαλάσσης ("and through the blast of your wrath the water was divided; the waters were frozen like a wall; the waves were frozen in the midst of the sea") — πνεῦμα θυμοῦ ("blast of wrath") for ruakh appekha — the LXX adds θυμός ("wrath"), making the anger explicit; τεῖχος ("wall") for ned; πήγνυμι ("freeze, solidify") for both ne'ermu and qafe'u.

**9** The enemy [oyev] said: 'I will pursue [erdof], I will overtake [assig], I will divide [akhalleq] plunder [shalal]; my nefesh [soul/appetite] will be filled [timla'emo] with them; I will draw [ariq] my sword [kharbi]; my hand [yadi] will dispossess them [torishemo].'

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אָמַר אוֹיֵב אֶרְדֹּף אַשִּׂיג אֲחַלֵּק שָׁלָל תִּמְלָאֵמוֹ נַפְשִׁי אָרִיק חַרְבִּי תּוֹרִישֵׁמוֹ יָדִי (amar oyev erdof assig akhalleq shalal timla'emo nafshi ariq kharbi torishemo yadi, "the enemy said: 'I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide plunder; my soul will be filled with them; I will draw my sword; my hand will dispossess them'"). **Key structure:** A rapid-fire sequence of six verbs in the first person — the boastful voice of the enemy, each verb a short, punching blow of arrogance: אֶרְדֹּף (erdof, "I will pursue"), אַשִּׂיג (assig, "I will overtake"), אֲחַלֵּק (akhalleq, "I will divide"), תִּמְלָאֵמוֹ (timla'emo, "it will fill"), אָרִיק (ariq, "I will draw/empty out"), תּוֹרִישֵׁמוֹ (torishemo, "it will dispossess"). The staccato rhythm mimics the breathless eagerness of pursuit. שָׁלָל (shalal) = "plunder, spoil, booty." The verb אָרִיק (ariq) = hiphil of ריק (riq, "to be empty") — "I will empty out, I will unsheathe/draw" (drawing a sword from its sheath = emptying the scabbard). תּוֹרִישֵׁמוֹ (torishemo) = hiphil of ירשׁ (yarash, "to possess, inherit, dispossess") — "it will dispossess/destroy them." LXX: εἶπεν ὁ ἐχθρός· διώξας καταλήμψομαι, μεριῶ σκῦλα, ἐμπλήσω ψυχήν μου, ἀνελῶ τῇ μαχαίρᾳ μου, κυριεύσει ἡ χείρ μου ("the enemy said: 'Having pursued I will overtake, I will divide spoils, I will fill my soul, I will slay with my sword, my hand will dominate'") — ἀναιρέω ("slay, destroy") for ariq kharbi — a more violent rendering; κυριεύω ("exercise lordship") for torishemo.

**10** You blew [nashafta] with your ruakh [wind/breath]; the sea [yam] covered them [kissamo]; they sank [tsalalu] like lead [ka-oferet] in the mighty [addirim] waters [be-mayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads נָשַׁפְתָּ בְרוּחֲךָ כִּסָּמוֹ יָם צָלְלוּ כַּעוֹפֶרֶת בְּמַיִם אַדִּירִים (nashafta be-rukhakha kissamo yam tsalalu ka-oferet be-mayim addirim, "you blew with your wind; the sea covered them; they sank like lead in the mighty waters"). **Key terms:** נָשַׁפְתָּ (nashafta) = qal perfect of נשׁף (nashaf, "to blow") — a rare verb, occurring only here and in Isaiah 40:24. The divine breath blew and the waters returned. כִּסָּמוֹ (kissamo) = piel of כסה (kasah, "to cover") + 3mp suffix — "it covered them." צָלְלוּ (tsalalu) = qal perfect of צלל (tsalal, "to sink, go deep") — "they sank." כַּעוֹפֶרֶת (ka-oferet) = "like lead" — עוֹפֶרֶת (oferet, "lead"), the heaviest common metal known to the ancient world. This is the third and climactic sinking simile: stone (v. 5), stubble (v. 7, consumed), lead (v. 10) — the heaviest and fastest. אַדִּירִים (addirim) = "mighty, majestic" — from אדר (adar, "to be great, glorious"); the same root as ne'dari in v. 6. The waters are not merely deep but addirim — mighty, overwhelming, majestic in their destructive power. LXX: ἀπέστειλας τὸ πνεῦμά σου, ἐκάλυψεν αὐτοὺς θάλασσα· ἔδυσαν ὡσεὶ μόλιβος ἐν ὕδατι σφοδρῷ ("you sent forth your wind; the sea covered them; they sank like lead in violent water") — μόλιβος ("lead") for oferet; σφοδρός ("violent, vehement") for addirim.

**11** Who [is] like you [mi kamokha] among the elim [gods], O YHWH? Who [is] like you — glorious [ne'dar] in qodesh [holiness], fearsome [nora] in praises [tehillot], doing wonder [feleh]?

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מִי־כָמֹכָה בָּאֵלִם יְהוָה מִי כָּמֹכָה נֶאְדָּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ נוֹרָא תְהִלֹּת עֹשֵׂה פֶלֶא (mi-kamokha ba-elim YHWH mi kamokha ne'dar ba-qodesh nora tehillot oseh fele, "who is like you among the gods, O YHWH? Who is like you, glorious in holiness, fearsome in praises, doing wonder?"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the incomparability formula:** מִי כָמֹכָה בָּאֵלִם (mi kamokha ba-elim) = "who is like you among the gods?" — from מִי (mi, "who?") + כְּ (ke, "like, as") + מוֹ (mo, archaic 2ms suffix) + כָה (kah, "thus"). The word אֵלִם (elim) = "gods, divine beings" — plural of אֵל (el, "god, mighty one"). This is not monotheism denying the existence of other gods but henotheistic/monolatrous praise declaring YHWH's incomparable supremacy among the divine council. The other elim exist in the singer's conceptual world, but none compare to YHWH. נֶאְדָּר (ne'dar) = niphal participle of אדר (adar, "to be glorious") — "made glorious, majestic." בַּקֹּדֶשׁ (ba-qodesh) = "in holiness" — from קדשׁ (qodesh, "holiness, separateness, sacredness"). YHWH's glory is rooted in His radical otherness (qodesh). נוֹרָא (nora) = niphal participle of ירא (yare, "to fear") — "to-be-feared, awe-inspiring, terrible." תְהִלֹּת (tehillot) = plural of תְּהִלָּה (tehillah, "praise, song of praise") — from הלל (halal, "to praise"). "Fearsome in praises" = the praises themselves inspire awe, or: "feared/revered [even] in songs of praise." פֶּלֶא (fele) = "wonder, marvel" — a singular noun emphasizing the extraordinary, incomprehensible nature of God's act. LXX: τίς ὅμοιός σοι ἐν θεοῖς, κύριε; τίς ὅμοιός σοι, δεδοξασμένος ἐν ἁγίοις, θαυμαστὸς ἐν δόξαις, ποιῶν τέρατα; ("who is like you among the gods, O Lord? Who is like you, glorified among the holy ones, wondrous in glories, doing wonders?") — ἐν ἁγίοις ("among the holy ones") for ba-qodesh — a significant shift from "in holiness" (abstract) to "among the holy ones" (angelic/divine beings), possibly reflecting a divine-council interpretation.

**12** You stretched out [natita] your right hand [yeminekha] — the earth [erets] swallowed them [tivla'emo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads נָטִיתָ יְמִינְךָ תִּבְלָעֵמוֹ אָרֶץ (natita yeminekha tivla'emo arets, "you stretched out your right hand — the earth swallowed them"). **Key terms:** נָטִיתָ (natita) = qal perfect of נטה (natah, "to stretch out, extend") — the same verb used for Mosheh stretching out his hand over the sea (14:21, 27). Now it is YHWH's own hand that stretches. תִּבְלָעֵמוֹ (tivla'emo) = qal imperfect of בלע (bala, "to swallow, engulf") + 3mp suffix — "it swallowed them." The earth (אֶרֶץ, erets) here functions as a synonym for the seabed or for Sheol — the underworld that opens to receive the dead. The image recalls Numbers 16:32 where the earth literally swallows Korah's company. The sea-bottom is the threshold of the netherworld. LXX: ἐξέτεινας τὴν δεξιάν σου, κατέπιεν αὐτοὺς γῆ ("you stretched out your right hand; the earth swallowed them") — καταπίνω ("swallow down") for tivla'emo.

**13** You led [nakhita] in your khesed [lovingkindness/steadfast love] this people [am-zu] which you redeemed [ga'alta]; you guided them [nehalta] in your strength [be-azzekha] to the habitation [neveh] of your qodesh [holiness].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads נָחִיתָ בְחַסְדְּךָ עַם־זוּ גָּאָלְתָּ נֵהַלְתָּ בְעָזְּךָ אֶל־נְוֵה קָדְשֶׁךָ (nakhita ve-khasdekha am-zu ga'alta neihalta be-azzekha el-neveh qodshekha, "you led in your lovingkindness this people which you redeemed; you guided in your strength to the habitation of your holiness"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — khesed and ge'ulah:** The verb נָחִיתָ (nakhita) = qal perfect of נחה (nakhah, "to lead, guide") — God as shepherd leading His flock. חֶסֶד (khesed) = "lovingkindness, steadfast love, covenant loyalty, faithful love" — one of the most theologically rich words in the Hebrew Bible, denoting God's enduring, committed, covenantal love that perseveres through all circumstances. It is not merely kindness but fierce, loyal, determined love. עַם־זוּ (am-zu) = "this people" — the demonstrative pronoun זוּ (zu) is archaic, found primarily in early poetry (cf. Ps 68:9; Isa 42:24; 43:21). גָּאַלְתָּ (ga'alta) = qal perfect of גאל (ga'al, "to redeem, act as kinsman-redeemer") — the verb of kinship redemption. The גֹּאֵל (go'el) is the near-kinsman who redeems family members from slavery, debt, or blood-guilt. YHWH is Yisra'el's go'el — their kinsman-redeemer. נֵהַלְתָּ (neihalta) = piel perfect of נהל (nahal, "to lead to a watering place, guide, refresh") — the verb specifically of leading flocks to water. נְוֵה (neveh) = "habitation, pasture, dwelling" — from נוה (navah, "to dwell"); the pastoral image of a settled, safe home. קָדְשְׁךָ (qodshekha) = "your holiness" — the habitation of YHWH's holiness is likely Mount Sinai or the promised land or the future Temple. LXX: ὡδήγησας τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ σου τὸν λαόν σου τοῦτον ὃν ἐλυτρώσω, παρεκάλεσας τῇ ἰσχύι σου εἰς κατάλυμα ἅγιόν σου ("you guided in your righteousness this people of yours whom you ransomed; you comforted in your strength unto your holy resting-place") — δικαιοσύνη ("righteousness") for khesed — a notable theological substitution; λυτρόω ("ransom") for ga'alta; κατάλυμα ("lodging, resting-place") for neveh.

**14** The peoples [ammim] heard [shame'u], they trembled [yirgazun]; khil [anguish/writhing] seized [akhaz] the inhabitants [yoshevei] of Pelashet [Philistia].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שָׁמְעוּ עַמִּים יִרְגָּזוּן חִיל אָחַז יֹשְׁבֵי פְּלָשֶׁת (shame'u ammim yirgazun khil akhaz yoshevei Pelashet, "the peoples heard, they trembled; anguish seized the inhabitants of Philistia"). **Key terms:** יִרְגָּזוּן (yirgazun) = qal imperfect of רגז (ragaz, "to tremble, quake, be agitated") with archaic -un ending — "they tremble." The -un suffix is characteristic of archaic Hebrew poetry. חִיל (khil) = "anguish, writhing, trembling, birth-pangs" — from חול/חיל (khul/khil, "to writhe, twist, tremble in pain"). The word evokes the twisting agony of a woman in labor. יֹשְׁבֵי פְּלָשֶׁת (yoshevei Pelashet) = "the inhabitants of Philistia" — the first of four neighboring nations whose terror is described (Philistia, Edom, Moab, Canaan, vv. 14–16). The geographical sequence traces the route from Egypt toward the promised land. LXX: ἤκουσαν ἔθνη καὶ ὠργίσθησαν, ὠδῖνες ἔλαβον κατοικοῦντας Φυλιστιιμ ("the nations heard and were angry; birth-pangs seized the inhabitants of Philistia") — ὠδίν ("birth-pang") for khil.

**15** Then the chiefs [allufei] of Edom were terrified [nivhalu]; the mighty ones [eilei] of Mo'av — trembling [ra'ad] seized them [yokhazemo]; all the inhabitants [yoshevei] of Kena'an [Canaan] melted away [namogu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אָז נִבְהֲלוּ אַלּוּפֵי אֱדוֹם אֵילֵי מוֹאָב יֹאחֲזֵמוֹ רָעַד נָמֹגוּ כֹּל יֹשְׁבֵי כְנָעַן (az nivhalu allufei Edom eilei Mo'av yokhazemo ra'ad namogu kol yoshevei Kena'an, "then the chiefs of Edom were terrified; the mighty ones of Mo'av — trembling seized them; all the inhabitants of Canaan melted away"). **Key terms:** אַלּוּפֵי (allufei) = plural construct of אַלּוּף (alluf, "chief, chieftain, clan-leader") — from אֶלֶף (elef, "thousand, clan"). אֵילֵי (eilei) = plural construct of אַיִל (ayil, "ram, mighty one, leader") — from the image of the lead ram of a flock; hence a powerful man, a leader. נִבְהֲלוּ (nivhalu) = niphal of בהל (bahal, "to be terrified, dismayed") — "they were terrified, they were panic-stricken." רָעַד (ra'ad) = "trembling, quaking" — from רעד (ra'ad, "to tremble"). נָמֹגוּ (namogu) = niphal of מוג (mug, "to melt, dissolve, faint") — "they melted away." The melting describes the dissolution of courage, the liquefaction of resolve in the face of YHWH's power (cf. Josh 2:9, 24 — Rahab: "all the inhabitants of the land melt before you"). LXX: τότε ἔσπευσαν ἡγεμόνες Εδωμ καὶ ἄρχοντες Μωαβιτῶν, ἔλαβεν αὐτοὺς τρόμος, ἐτάκησαν πάντες οἱ κατοικοῦντες Χανααν ("then the rulers of Edom hurried, and the rulers of the Moabites — trembling seized them; all the inhabitants of Canaan melted") — τήκω ("melt") for namogu.

**16** Terror and dread [eimata va-fakhad] fall [tippol] upon them; by the greatness [bi-gdol] of your arm [zero'akha] they are still [yidemu] like a stone [ka-aven] — until your people [ammekha] pass over [ya'avor], O YHWH, until this people [am-zu] pass over which you acquired [qanita].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads תִּפֹּל עֲלֵיהֶם אֵימָתָה וָפַחַד בִּגְדֹל זְרוֹעֲךָ יִדְּמוּ כָּאָבֶן עַד־יַעֲבֹר עַמְּךָ יְהוָה עַד־יַעֲבֹר עַם־זוּ קָנִיתָ (tippol aleihem eimatah va-fakhad bi-gdol zero'akha yiddemu ka-aven ad-ya'avor ammekha YHWH ad-ya'avor am-zu qanita, "terror and dread fall upon them; by the greatness of your arm they are still like a stone — until your people pass over, O YHWH, until this people pass over which you acquired"). **Key terms:** אֵימָתָה (eimatah) = "terror, dread" — from אימה (eimah, "terror, fear"); the -tah ending is an archaic feminine suffix. פַּחַד (fakhad) = "dread, fear, trembling" — the doubling of terror-words (eimatah + fakhad) intensifies the paralyzing fear. זְרוֹעַ (zero'a) = "arm" — the arm of YHWH, the instrument of divine power (cf. Deut 4:34; 5:15; 26:8). יִדְּמוּ (yiddemu) = qal imperfect of דמם (damam, "to be still, silent, motionless") — "they are still, they are struck dumb, they are petrified." כָּאָבֶן (ka-aven) = "like a stone" — recalling v. 5; the enemies are frozen, petrified, stone-like in their terror. The repeated עַד־יַעֲבֹר (ad-ya'avor) = "until [they] pass over" — from עבר (avar, "to cross over, pass through"). This passing-over anticipates the crossing of the Yarden (Jordan). קָנִיתָ (qanita) = qal perfect of קנה (qanah, "to acquire, buy, create, possess") — "which you acquired/created." The verb qanah can mean both "acquire" (by purchase) and "create" (cf. Gen 14:19; Prov 8:22). YHWH has acquired/created this people as His own possession. LXX: ἐπιπέσοι ἐπ᾽ αὐτοὺς φόβος καὶ τρόμος· μεγέθει βραχίονός σου ἀπολιθωθήτωσαν, ἕως ἂν παρέλθῃ ὁ λαός σου, κύριε, ἕως ἂν παρέλθῃ ὁ λαός σου οὗτος ὃν ἐκτήσω ("let fear and trembling fall upon them; by the greatness of your arm let them be turned to stone, until your people pass by, O Lord, until this people of yours pass by whom you acquired") — ἀπολιθόω ("turn to stone, petrify") for yiddemu ka-aven — a striking rendering that makes the stone-simile into a literal petrification.

**17** You bring them [tevi'emo] and plant them [ve-titta'emo] on the mountain [be-har] of your inheritance [nakhalatekha], the established-place [makhon] of your dwelling [le-shivtekha] [which] you have made [pa'alta], O YHWH — the miqdash [sanctuary], O Adonai, [which] your hands [yadekha] established [konenu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads תְּבִאֵמוֹ וְתִטָּעֵמוֹ בְּהַר נַחֲלָתְךָ מָכוֹן לְשִׁבְתְּךָ פָּעַלְתָּ יְהוָה מִקְּדָשׁ אֲדֹנָי כּוֹנְנוּ יָדֶיךָ (tevi'emo ve-titta'emo be-har nakhalatekha makhon le-shivtekha pa'alta YHWH miqdash Adonai konenu yadekha, "you bring them and plant them on the mountain of your inheritance, the established-place of your dwelling [which] you have made, O YHWH — the sanctuary, O Lord, [which] your hands established"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the mountain of inheritance:** The verb תְּבִאֵמוֹ (tevi'emo) = hiphil imperfect of בוא (bo, "to come, enter") + 3mp suffix — "you bring them in." וְתִטָּעֵמוֹ (ve-titta'emo) = qal imperfect of נטע (nata, "to plant") + 3mp suffix — "and you plant them." The planting metaphor: Yisra'el is a tree to be planted by YHWH on His mountain. הַר נַחֲלָתְךָ (har nakhalatekha) = "the mountain of your inheritance" — from הַר (har, "mountain") + נַחֲלָה (nakhalah, "inheritance, possession, heritage"). The "mountain of YHWH's inheritance" could refer to Mount Sinai, Mount Tsiyon (Zion), or the entire promised land envisioned as YHWH's mountain domain. מָכוֹן (makhon) = "established place, foundation, fixed place" — from כון (kun, "to be established, be firm"). This is the prepared, fixed, immovable dwelling-place. לְשִׁבְתְּךָ (le-shivtekha) = "for your dwelling/sitting" — from ישׁב (yashav, "to sit, dwell"). פָּעַלְתָּ (pa'alta) = qal perfect of פעל (pa'al, "to make, do, work") — "you made/fashioned." מִקְּדָשׁ (miqdash) = "sanctuary, holy place" — from קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy"). כּוֹנְנוּ (konenu) = polel perfect of כון (kun) — "they established, they founded." יָדֶיךָ (yadekha) = "your hands" — dual of יָד (yad, "hand"). The sanctuary is the work of YHWH's own hands — not built by human craftsmen but established by God Himself. LXX: εἰσαγαγὼν καταφύτευσον αὐτοὺς εἰς ὄρος κληρονομίας σου, εἰς ἕτοιμον κατοικητήριόν σου ὃ κατειργάσω, κύριε, ἁγίασμα, κύριε, ὃ ἡτοίμασαν αἱ χεῖρές σου ("having brought in, plant them on the mountain of your inheritance, into the prepared dwelling of yours which you fashioned, O Lord — the sanctuary, O Lord, which your hands prepared") — κληρονομία ("inheritance") for nakhalah; ἁγίασμα ("sanctuary, holy thing") for miqdash.

**18** YHWH will reign [yimlokh] le-olam va'ed [forever and ever]!

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads יְהוָה יִמְלֹךְ לְעֹלָם וָעֶד (YHWH yimlokh le-olam va-ed, "YHWH will reign forever and ever"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the eternal kingship:** The verb יִמְלֹךְ (yimlokh) = qal imperfect of מלך (malakh, "to reign, be king") — "will reign / reigns." The Song's climax is not a military victory statement but a theological declaration of eternal sovereignty. YHWH is not merely a warrior who won a battle; He is the eternal King. The phrase לְעֹלָם וָעֶד (le-olam va-ed) = "to/for forever and ever" — from עוֹלָם (olam, "perpetuity, eternity, age") + עַד (ad, "perpetuity, duration"). The doubling of eternal terms emphasizes the boundless, unending nature of YHWH's reign. This verse is the theological capstone of the Song: the God who defeats Egypt's gods and army is the God who reigns without end. LXX: κύριος βασιλεύων τὸν αἰῶνα καὶ ἐπ᾽ αἰῶνα καὶ ἔτι ("the Lord reigning unto the age and upon the age and still") — βασιλεύω ("reign as king") for yimlokh; the LXX adds καὶ ἔτι ("and still/further"), extending the formula beyond the Hebrew.

**19** For the horse [sus] of Par'oh came [ba] with his chariot [be-rikhbo] and with his horsemen [u-ve-farashav] into the sea, and YHWH turned back [vayashev] upon them the waters of the sea; and the sons of Yisra'el walked [halakhu] on dry ground [ba-yabashah] in the midst of [be-tokh] the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי בָא סוּס פַּרְעֹה בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו בַּיָּם וַיָּשֶׁב יְהוָה עֲלֵהֶם אֶת־מֵי הַיָּם וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָלְכוּ בַיַּבָּשָׁה בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם (ki va sus Par'oh be-rikhbo u-ve-farashav ba-yam vayashev YHWH aleihem et-mei ha-yam u-venei Yisra'el halakhu va-yabashah be-tokh ha-yam, "for the horse of Par'oh came with his chariot and with his horsemen into the sea, and YHWH turned back upon them the waters of the sea; and the sons of Yisra'el walked on dry ground in the midst of the sea"). This prose verse serves as a hinge between the Song and the narrative, recapitulating the core event in plain narrative language: Par'oh's forces entered the sea; YHWH returned the waters; Yisra'el walked through on dry ground. The singular סוּס (sus, "horse") is collective — all Par'oh's cavalry. The verb וַיָּשֶׁב (vayashev) = hiphil of שׁוב (shuv, "to return, turn back") — "He caused to return, He turned back." YHWH reversed the waters. LXX: ὅτι εἰσῆλθεν ἵππος Φαραω σὺν ἅρμασιν καὶ ἀναβάταις εἰς θάλασσαν, καὶ ἐπήγαγεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτοὺς κύριος τὸ ὕδωρ τῆς θαλάσσης· οἱ δὲ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐπορεύθησαν διὰ ξηρᾶς ἐν μέσῳ τῆς θαλάσσης ("for the horse of Pharao with chariots and riders entered into the sea, and the Lord brought back upon them the water of the sea; but the sons of Israel went through dry [ground] in the midst of the sea").

## The Song of Miriam (vv. 20–21)

**20** And Miriam the neviah [prophetess], the sister of Aharon, took the tof [tambourine/hand-drum] in her hand; and all the women went out after her with tuppim [tambourines] and with mekholot [dances].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַתִּקַּח מִרְיָם הַנְּבִיאָה אֲחוֹת אַהֲרֹן אֶת־הַתֹּף בְּיָדָהּ וַתֵּצֶאןָ כָל־הַנָּשִׁים אַחֲרֶיהָ בְּתֻפִּים וּבִמְחֹלֹת (vattiqqakh Miriam ha-nevi'ah akhot Aharon et-ha-tof be-yadah vattetsena khol-ha-nashim achareiha be-tuppim u-vi-mkholot, "and Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aharon, took the tambourine in her hand; and all the women went out after her with tambourines and with dances"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — Miriam ha-nevi'ah:** מִרְיָם (Miriam) — the first woman in the Torah explicitly called הַנְּבִיאָה (ha-nevi'ah, "the prophetess"), the feminine form of נָבִיא (navi, "prophet"). She is identified as אֲחוֹת אַהֲרֹן (akhot Aharon, "sister of Aharon") — notably not "sister of Mosheh." This may reflect an early tradition in which Miriam and Aharon shared a special bond, or it may distinguish her from other Miriams, or it may reflect the priestly lineage. תֹּף (tof) = "tambourine, hand-drum, timbrel" — a small frame drum, the primary rhythmic instrument of women's celebratory music in ancient Israel (cf. Judg 11:34; 1 Sam 18:6; 2 Sam 6:5). תֻּפִּים (tuppim) = plural. מְחֹלֹת (mekholot) = "dances, round-dances" — from מחל/חול (makhal/khul, "to dance, whirl"). The women's response is embodied worship — drums and dance, a full-body expression of deliverance. LXX: λαβοῦσα δὲ Μαριαμ ἡ προφῆτις ἡ ἀδελφὴ Ααρων τὸ τύμπανον ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτῆς, καὶ ἐξήλθοσαν πᾶσαι αἱ γυναῖκες ὀπίσω αὐτῆς μετὰ τυμπάνων καὶ χορῶν ("and Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, having taken the tambourine in her hand, and all the women went out after her with tambourines and dances") — τύμπανον ("drum, tambourine") for tof; χορός ("dance, chorus") for mekholot.

**21** And Miriam answered [vatta'an] to them: "Sing [shiru] to YHWH, for he has risen in triumph [ga'oh ga'ah] — the horse [sus] and its rider [rokhevo] He has hurled [ramah] into the sea [ba-yam]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַתַּעַן לָהֶם מִרְיָם שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה כִּי־גָאֹה גָּאָה סוּס וְרֹכְבוֹ רָמָה בַיָּם (vatta'an lahem Miriam shiru la-YHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus ve-rokhevo ramah va-yam, "and Miriam answered to them: 'Sing to YHWH, for He has risen in triumph — the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea'"). **Key terms:** וַתַּעַן (vatta'an) = qal imperfect (wayyiqtol) of ענה (anah, "to answer, respond, sing responsively") — "she answered/responded." The verb anah in musical contexts means antiphonal response — Miriam leads a call-and-response song. The pronoun לָהֶם (lahem) = "to them" (masculine plural) — suggesting she sang to the entire assembly, not only to the women. שִׁירוּ (shiru) = imperative masculine plural of שׁיר (shir, "to sing") — "sing!" (addressed to the whole people). Miriam's song repeats the refrain of v. 1b verbatim, except that the opening אָשִׁירָה (ashirah, "I will sing," first-person cohortative) is replaced by שִׁירוּ (shiru, "sing!" second-person imperative plural). Many scholars consider this brief couplet the original, oldest form of the victory song, later expanded into the full Song of vv. 1–18. LXX: καὶ ἐξῆρχεν αὐτοῖς Μαριαμ λέγουσα· ᾄσωμεν τῷ κυρίῳ, ἐνδόξως γὰρ δεδόξασται· ἵππον καὶ ἀναβάτην ἔρριψεν εἰς θάλασσαν ("and Miriam led them, saying: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for He has been gloriously glorified; horse and rider He has cast into the sea'") — ἐξάρχω ("lead, begin the song") for vatta'an — the LXX understands Miriam as the song-leader, the one who initiates the singing.

## The Waters of Marah (vv. 22–27)

**22** And Mosheh caused Yisra'el to journey [vayassa] from the Yam Suf [Sea of Reeds], and they went out [vayetse'u] to the midbar [wilderness] of Shur; and they walked [vayelekhu] three days [sheloshet yamim] in the midbar [wilderness] and did not find [ve-lo matse'u] water [mayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּסַּע מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִיַּם־סוּף וַיֵּצְאוּ אֶל־מִדְבַּר־שׁוּר וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁלֹשֶׁת־יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר וְלֹא־מָצְאוּ מָיִם (vayassa Mosheh et-Yisra'el mi-Yam-Suf vayetse'u el-midbar-Shur vayelekhu sheloshet-yamim ba-midbar ve-lo-matse'u mayim, "and Mosheh caused Yisra'el to journey from the Sea of Reeds, and they went out to the wilderness of Shur; and they walked three days in the wilderness and did not find water"). **Key terms:** וַיַּסַּע (vayassa) = hiphil of נסע (nasa, "to pull up [stakes], journey, set out") — "he caused to journey / he led forth." Mosheh is the active agent moving the people forward. שׁוּר (Shur) = "wall" — the wilderness region east of Egypt, named perhaps for the Egyptian border wall/fortification system. The transition from ecstatic victory song (vv. 1–21) to desperate thirst (vv. 22–27) is abrupt and theologically significant: the life of faith alternates between mountaintop praise and wilderness testing. שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים (sheloshet yamim) = "three days" — the same period Mosheh originally requested from Par'oh (3:18; 5:3; 8:27). LXX: ἐξῆρεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἀπὸ θαλάσσης ἐρυθρᾶς καὶ ἤγαγεν αὐτοὺς εἰς τὴν ἔρημον Σουρ· καὶ ἐπορεύοντο τρεῖς ἡμέρας ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ καὶ οὐχ ηὕρισκον ὕδωρ ("and Mosheh lifted up the sons of Israel from the Red Sea and led them into the wilderness of Sour; and they journeyed three days in the wilderness and did not find water").

**23** And they came [vayavo'u] to Marah, and they were not able [ve-lo yakhlu] to drink [lishtot] water [mayim] from Marah, for they [were] bitter [marim]; therefore [al-ken] its name was called [qara shemah] Marah.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹאוּ מָרָתָה וְלֹא יָכְלוּ לִשְׁתֹּת מַיִם מִמָּרָה כִּי מָרִים הֵם עַל־כֵּן קָרָא־שְׁמָהּ מָרָה (vayavo'u Maratah ve-lo yakhlu lishtot mayim mi-Marah ki marim hem al-ken qara-shemah Marah, "and they came to Marah, and they were not able to drink water from Marah, for they [were] bitter; therefore its name was called Marah"). **Key terms:** מָרָה (Marah) = "Bitter" — from מרר (marar, "to be bitter"). The place-name is an etiology: the waters are bitter, so the place is named "Bitterness." The adjective מָרִים (marim) = plural of מַר (mar, "bitter") — grammatically modifying the waters (mayim, masc. pl.). The wordplay is concentrated: Marah (place-name) + marim (description) + Marah (repeated). The bitterness is literal (undrinkable water) and symbolic (the bitter testing of the wilderness). The suffix -ה (he-locale) on מָרָתָה (Maratah) indicates direction — "toward Marah." LXX: ἦλθον δὲ εἰς Μερρα, καὶ οὐκ ἠδύναντο πιεῖν ἐκ Μερρας, πικρὸν γὰρ ἦν· διὰ τοῦτο ἐπωνομάσθη τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου Πικρία ("and they came to Merra, and they were not able to drink from Merra, for it was bitter; therefore the name of that place was called Bitterness") — Πικρία ("Bitterness") for Marah — the LXX translates the place-name.

**24** And the people grumbled [vayillonu] against Mosheh, saying: "What shall we drink [mah-nishteh]?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּלֹּנוּ הָעָם עַל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר מַה־נִּשְׁתֶּה (vayillonu ha-am al-Mosheh lemor mah-nishteh, "and the people grumbled against Mosheh, saying: 'What shall we drink?'"). **Key terms:** וַיִּלֹּנוּ (vayillonu) = niphal (or hiphil) of לון (lun, "to grumble, murmur, complain") — "they grumbled." This verb becomes the technical term for wilderness complaint (cf. 16:2, 7, 8; 17:3; Num 14:2, 36; 16:11, 41; 17:5). The people direct their complaint against Mosheh (עַל־מֹשֶׁה, al-Mosheh) — the human mediator bears the brunt of the frustration meant for God. The question מַה־נִּשְׁתֶּה (mah-nishteh) = "what shall we drink?" — from שׁתה (shatah, "to drink"). A raw, existential cry: three days without water in the desert, and the first water they find is undrinkable. The Song's theology (vv. 1–21) meets material reality. LXX: καὶ διεγόγγυζεν ὁ λαὸς ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν λέγοντες· τί πιόμεθα; ("and the people murmured against Mosheh, saying: 'What shall we drink?'") — διαγογγύζω ("murmur, grumble") for vayillonu.

**25** And he cried out [vayits'aq] to YHWH, and YHWH showed him [vayorehu] a tree [ets]; and he cast [vayashilekh] [it] into the waters [el-ha-mayim], and the waters became sweet [vayimtequ]. There He set [sam] for him [lo] a khoq [statute] and a mishpat [judgment], and there He tested him [nissahu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּצְעַק אֶל־יְהוָה וַיּוֹרֵהוּ יְהוָה עֵץ וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶל־הַמַּיִם וַיִּמְתְּקוּ הַמָּיִם שָׁם שָׂם לוֹ חֹק וּמִשְׁפָּט וְשָׁם נִסָּהוּ (vayits'aq el-YHWH vayorehu YHWH ets vayashilekh el-ha-mayim vayimtequ ha-mayim sham sam lo khoq u-mishpat ve-sham nissahu, "and he cried out to YHWH, and YHWH showed him a tree; and he cast [it] into the waters, and the waters became sweet. There He set for him a statute and a judgment, and there He tested him"). **Key terms:** וַיּוֹרֵהוּ (vayorehu) = hiphil of ירה (yarah, "to throw, cast; to teach, instruct, show") + 3ms suffix — "He showed him / He instructed him / He pointed out to him." The same root that gives us תּוֹרָה (Torah, "instruction, law, teaching"). YHWH "torahs" Mosheh — instructs him by pointing to a tree. עֵץ (ets) = "tree, wood" — a specific tree or piece of wood that YHWH identifies. The text does not specify the species. וַיִּמְתְּקוּ (vayimtequ) = qal imperfect of מתק (mataq, "to be sweet, become sweet") — "they became sweet." The bitter (mar) is transformed to sweet (matoq) by divine intervention through natural means (a tree). חֹק (khoq) = "statute, decree, prescribed limit" — from חקק (khaqaq, "to engrave, inscribe, decree"). מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) = "judgment, ordinance, justice, legal decision" — from שׁפט (shafat, "to judge"). These two terms together (khoq u-mishpat) represent the fullness of divine law. נִסָּהוּ (nissahu) = piel of נסה (nasah, "to test, try, prove") + 3ms suffix — "He tested him." The wilderness is a place of testing — God tests Yisra'el's faithfulness and obedience. LXX: ἐβόησεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς κύριον, καὶ ἔδειξεν αὐτῷ κύριος ξύλον, καὶ ἐνέβαλεν αὐτὸ εἰς τὸ ὕδωρ, καὶ ἐγλυκάνθη τὸ ὕδωρ. ἐκεῖ ἔθετο αὐτῷ δικαιώματα καὶ κρίσεις, καὶ ἐκεῖ ἐπείρασεν αὐτόν ("and Mosheh cried out to the Lord, and the Lord showed him a tree, and he cast it into the water, and the water was made sweet. There He set for him ordinances and judgments, and there He tested him") — ξύλον ("wood, tree") for ets; γλυκαίνω ("make sweet") for vayimtequ; πειράζω ("test, try") for nissahu.

**26** And He said: "If you will diligently listen [shamo'a tishma] to the voice [le-qol] of YHWH your Elohim [God], and you do the right [ha-yashar] in His eyes, and you give ear [ve-ha'azanta] to His commandments [le-mitsvotav], and you keep [ve-shamarta] all His statutes [khuqqotav] — every sickness [ha-makhalah] which I put [samti] upon Mitsrayim I will not put upon you, for I [am] YHWH your healer [rofekha]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אִם־שָׁמוֹעַ תִּשְׁמַע לְקוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ וְהַיָּשָׁר בְּעֵינָיו תַּעֲשֶׂה וְהַאֲזַנְתָּ לְמִצְוֹתָיו וְשָׁמַרְתָּ כָּל־חֻקָּיו כָּל־הַמַּחֲלָה אֲשֶׁר־שַׂמְתִּי בְמִצְרַיִם לֹא־אָשִׂים עָלֶיךָ כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה רֹפְאֶךָ (vayomer im-shamo'a tishma le-qol YHWH Elohekha ve-ha-yashar be-einav ta'aseh ve-ha'azanta le-mitsvotav ve-shamarta kol-khuqqav kol-ha-makhalah asher-samti ve-Mitsrayim lo-asim alekha ki ani YHWH rofekha, "and He said: 'If diligently you listen to the voice of YHWH your God, and the right in His eyes you do, and you give ear to His commandments, and you keep all His statutes — every sickness which I put upon Mitsrayim I will not put upon you, for I [am] YHWH your healer'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — YHWH Rofekha:** The construction שָׁמוֹעַ תִּשְׁמַע (shamo'a tishma) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of שׁמע (shama, "to hear, listen, obey") — "hearing you shall hear / you shall diligently listen." The cognate accusative intensifies: total, attentive, obedient listening. The conditional structure (אִם, im, "if") establishes the covenant pattern: obedience leads to divine protection. הַיָּשָׁר (ha-yashar) = "the right, the straight, the upright" — from ישׁר (yashar, "to be straight, right"). וְהַאֲזַנְתָּ (ve-ha'azanta) = hiphil of אזן (azan, "to give ear, listen attentively") — from אֹזֶן (ozen, "ear"). מִצְוֹת (mitsvot) = "commandments" — from צוה (tsavah, "to command"). חֻקִּים (khuqqim) = "statutes" — from חקק (khaqaq, "to engrave"). מַחֲלָה (makhalah) = "sickness, disease" — from חלה (khalah, "to be sick, weak"). The plagues of Egypt were מַחֲלוֹת (makhalot) — diseases sent by YHWH. The climactic title: אֲנִי יְהוָה רֹפְאֶךָ (ani YHWH rofekha) = "I [am] YHWH your healer" — from רפא (rafa, "to heal, cure, restore"). The participle רֹפֵא (rofe) = "healer, physician." YHWH is the divine physician. The one who inflicted the plagues is also the one who heals — the same God who made the waters bitter makes them sweet, who sent sickness also grants health. LXX: ἐὰν ἀκοῇ ἀκούσῃς τῆς φωνῆς κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ σου καὶ τὰ ἀρεστὰ ἐναντίον αὐτοῦ ποιήσῃς καὶ ἐνωτίσῃ ταῖς ἐντολαῖς αὐτοῦ καὶ φυλάξῃς πάντα τὰ δικαιώματα αὐτοῦ, πᾶσαν νόσον ἣν ἐπήγαγον τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις οὐκ ἐπάξω ἐπὶ σέ· ἐγὼ γάρ εἰμι κύριος ὁ ἰώμενός σε ("if by hearing you hear the voice of the Lord your God and do the things pleasing before Him and give ear to His commandments and keep all His ordinances, every disease which I brought upon the Egyptians I will not bring upon you; for I am the Lord who heals you") — ἰάομαι ("heal, cure") for rofekha.

**27** And they came [vayavo'u] to Eilim [Elim], and there [were] twelve [shtem esreh] springs [einot] of water [mayim] and seventy [shiv'im] palm trees [temarim]; and they encamped [vayakhanu] there by the waters [al-ha-mayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹאוּ אֵילִמָה וְשָׁם שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה עֵינֹת מַיִם וְשִׁבְעִים תְּמָרִים וַיַּחֲנוּ־שָׁם עַל־הַמָּיִם (vayavo'u Eilimah ve-sham shtem esreh einot mayim ve-shiv'im temarim vayakhanu-sham al-ha-mayim, "and they came to Elim, and there [were] twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees; and they encamped there by the waters"). **Key terms:** אֵילִם (Eilim) = possibly "great trees, terebinths" — from אַיִל (ayil, "ram, great tree, mighty one"); the plural suggests a grove of great trees. The suffix -ה (he-locale) on אֵילִמָה (Eilimah) indicates direction: "toward Elim." עֵינֹת (einot) = plural of עַיִן (ayin, "eye, spring, fountain") — a natural spring, literally an "eye" of water in the ground. שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה (shtem esreh) = "twelve" — corresponding to the twelve tribes of Yisra'el. שִׁבְעִים (shiv'im) = "seventy" — corresponding to the seventy elders (cf. Num 11:16, 24–25). תְּמָרִים (temarim) = plural of תָּמָר (tamar, "palm tree, date palm") — the iconic tree of desert oases. The numbers are symbolically loaded: twelve springs for twelve tribes, seventy palms for the seventy elders or the seventy nations. After the bitter waters of Marah, Elim is an oasis of abundance — divine provision following divine testing. The chapter ends with rest and water, a foretaste of the promised land. LXX: καὶ ἤλθοσαν εἰς Αιλιμ· καὶ ἦσαν ἐκεῖ δώδεκα πηγαὶ ὑδάτων καὶ ἑβδομήκοντα στελέχη φοινίκων· παρενέβαλον δὲ ἐκεῖ παρὰ τὰ ὕδατα ("and they came to Ailim; and there were there twelve springs of waters and seventy trunks of palm trees; and they encamped there by the waters") — πηγή ("spring, fountain") for einot; στέλεχος φοίνικος ("trunk of a palm") for temarim.

## Theological Summary

The Song of the Sea (Shirat ha-Yam) is the oldest extended poem in the Torah and the theological climax of the exodus narrative. Its three movements — (1) YHWH as divine warrior destroying Egypt at the sea (vv. 1–12), (2) YHWH as shepherd leading His redeemed people to His mountain-sanctuary (vv. 13–17), and (3) the declaration of YHWH's eternal kingship (v. 18) — together compose the foundational creed of Israelite faith. The God who fights, leads, and reigns is one and the same YHWH.

Key theological threads:

- **Divine Warrior (ish milkhamah):** YHWH is identified directly as a warrior (v. 3). His weapons are the cosmic elements — wind/breath (ruakh), sea (yam), and the primordial deep (tehom). The battle is entirely YHWH's; Yisra'el does nothing but sing.
- **The incomparability of YHWH (mi kamokha):** The rhetorical question of v. 11 acknowledges the existence of other elim (gods/divine beings) while declaring YHWH utterly beyond comparison — glorious in holiness, fearsome in praises, a doer of wonders.
- **Khesed and ge'ulah (vv. 13):** The warrior-God is also the covenant-loyal redeemer (go'el) who leads in steadfast love (khesed). Power and tenderness converge.
- **The mountain of inheritance (v. 17):** The Song looks forward — beyond the sea, beyond the wilderness — to the planting of Yisra'el on YHWH's mountain, in YHWH's sanctuary. The exodus is not merely escape but arrival.
- **YHWH yimlokh le-olam va-ed (v. 18):** The climax is not military but monarchical — YHWH reigns forever. The exodus reveals not just power but sovereignty.
- **Miriam ha-nevi'ah (vv. 20–21):** The first woman called "prophetess" leads the embodied, communal, musical response — drum, dance, and antiphonal song.
- **YHWH Rofekha (v. 26):** At Marah, the warrior-king reveals Himself as healer. The God who smites Egypt also heals Israel. Obedience is the condition; divine healing is the promise.
- **From Marah to Elim (vv. 23–27):** The wilderness pattern is established: testing (Marah) followed by provision (Elim). Bitter water made sweet. Twelve springs for twelve tribes.
